cards.front: What are the two main categories of program-defined types covered in Chapter 13?
cards.back:
• Enumerations: Named symbolic constants (enum, enum class)
  • Structs/Classes: Aggregate types with multiple members

cards.text: Prefer {{c1::enum class}} over unscoped enum, and prefer {{c2::structs with default member initialization}} to avoid uninitialized members.

cards.front: What's the key difference between enum and enum class?
cards.back: enum class is scoped (enumerators don't pollute namespace) and doesn't implicitly convert to int, providing better type safety.

cards.front: What are the three ways to initialize struct members?
cards.back:
• Aggregate initialization: { val1, val2 }
  • Designated initializers (C++20): { .member = val }
  • Default member initialization: in struct definition

cards.text: Use {{c1::.}} (dot) for objects/references, and {{c2::->}} (arrow) for pointers when accessing struct members.

cards.front: What are the two template-related features that reduce boilerplate?
cards.back:
• CTAD (C++17): Deduces template arguments from initializers
  • Alias templates: Create shortcuts for complex template types

cards.explanation:
Main takeaways:
  • Use enum class for type-safe enumerations
  • Always initialize struct members
  • Pass large structs by const reference
  • Use templates for type-safe generic code
  • Leverage CTAD and alias templates for cleaner code
examples: struct Point { int x = 0; int y = 0; };
template<typename T> struct Box { T val; };

cards.front: How do you return multiple values from a function idiomatically?
cards.back: Return a struct with named members. More readable than std::pair/tuple, and efficient with RVO.

cards.text: To enable custom I/O for types, overload operators {{c1::<<}} (output) and {{c2::>>}} (input).
The two main program-defined types are {{c1::enumerations}} (named constants) and {{c2::structs/classes}} (aggregate types).
Three struct initialization methods: {{c1::aggregate}} initialization ({ val1, val2 }), {{c2::designated}} initializers (C++20), and {{c3::default member}} initialization.
The two main program-defined types are {{c1::enumerations}} (named constants) and {{c2::structs/classes}} (aggregate types).
